root.doc: Metamorphic facies defined by Pressure-Temperature conditions (Eskola 1915, Turner 1948). Note: This is distinct from Stratigraphy.SedimentaryFacies which represents depositional environments. Metamorphic facies are defined by stable mineral assemblages at specific P-T conditions: zeolite, prehnite-pumpellyite, greenschist, blueschist, amphibolite, granulite, eclogite, hornfels, sanidinite.
root.name: MetamorphicFacies

root.doc: Sedimentary facies for depositional environment interpretation. Note: This is distinct from Petrology.MetamorphicFacies which represents P-T conditions. Sedimentary facies are defined by lithology, sedimentary structures, and fossils (Walther's Law applies). Types: lithofacies (rock character), biofacies (fossil content), ichnofacies (trace fossils), seismic_facies (reflection patterns).
root.name: SedimentaryFacies

Structural data informs rock mass characterization
Mineral data used in rock classification

Well samples analyzed for water chemistry

audience_notes.auditor: Defines the transactional boundary — all changes within an aggregate are atomic and auditable as a single unit.
audience_notes.data_steward: Each aggregate owns its data lifecycle. Cross-aggregate references use UUIDs, not foreign keys.
audience_notes.developer: Maps to CommandService + CommandRepository in generated code. One PostgreSQL transaction per aggregate write.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Represents a business concept that must always be consistent as a whole. For example, an Order and its OrderItems form one aggregate.
audience_notes.new_team_member: Think of it as a 'consistency bubble' — everything inside must be valid at all times.
audience_notes.project_manager: Each aggregate becomes a self-contained module with its own API endpoints, tests, and database tables.
audience_notes.security_officer: Access control is enforced at the aggregate boundary — no partial reads or writes bypass the service layer.

ddd_glossary.definition: A cluster of domain objects treated as a single unit for data changes. External references are limited to the root entity. All invariants within the aggregate are enforced transactionally.

audience_notes.auditor: Defines the scope of a regulatory domain — audit trails are context-scoped.
audience_notes.data_steward: Data ownership boundaries. Each context is the single source of truth for its entities.
audience_notes.developer: Maps to a shared library (OSLPC). Each context gets its own namespace, database schema, and gRPC service.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Each bounded context speaks its own language. 'Patient' in clinical care is different from 'Patient' in billing.
audience_notes.new_team_member: Think of it as a team boundary — each context has its own vocabulary and rules.
audience_notes.project_manager: Contexts can be developed, deployed, and scaled independently by different teams.
audience_notes.security_officer: Security policies are context-scoped. Cross-context communication goes through explicit interfaces.

ddd_glossary.definition: A linguistic and conceptual boundary within which a domain model is defined and applicable. The same real-world concept may have different representations in different bounded contexts.

audience_notes.auditor: Each entity has a stable UUID that persists across all changes — every modification is traceable.
audience_notes.data_steward: Entities define the canonical schema. All field types, constraints, and documentation are model-driven.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as a C++ struct with UUID primary key, PostgreSQL table, and gRPC message type.
audience_notes.domain_expert: A real-world thing with a unique identity. A Patient entity tracks the same person across all interactions.
audience_notes.new_team_member: The basic building blocks — like rows in a database table, but with business rules attached.
audience_notes.project_manager: Entities are the core data units. Each entity generates a database table, API endpoint, and UI form.
audience_notes.security_officer: Entity-level access control and field-level encryption are supported through generated code.

ddd_glossary.definition: An object defined primarily by its identity (UUID), rather than its attributes. Entities have a lifecycle and can change state over time while remaining the same entity.

audience_notes.auditor: Value objects are immutable — once created, they cannot be changed, only replaced. This simplifies audit trails.
audience_notes.data_steward: Define complex attribute groups with their own validation rules and documentation.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as inline fields (owned) or separate table (shared). Storage mode configurable in DSL.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Represents a concept with no separate identity — like an Address or a MoneyAmount. Two identical addresses are the same.
audience_notes.new_team_member: Like a struct that's compared by its values, not by some ID. Think: postal address, GPS coordinate.
audience_notes.project_manager: Reusable data structures shared across entities. Reduces duplication in the model.
audience_notes.security_officer: Immutability guarantees no partial updates. Value objects are either fully valid or rejected.

ddd_glossary.definition: An immutable object defined by its attributes rather than identity. Two value objects with the same attributes are considered equal. Used to encapsulate complex attribute groups.

audience_notes.auditor: Events form an immutable audit trail. Every state change is recorded as a timestamped fact.
audience_notes.data_steward: Events are the source of truth for what happened and when. They carry structured payload data.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as PostgreSQL event table, gRPC event message, and C++ event struct with payload fields.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Captures business-significant things that happened: 'OrderPlaced', 'PaymentReceived', 'PatientAdmitted'.
audience_notes.new_team_member: Think of a notification — 'this thing just happened' — that gets stored permanently.
audience_notes.project_manager: Events enable integration between contexts and support future analytics requirements.
audience_notes.security_officer: Events are append-only and tamper-evident. They provide a complete history of all system changes.

ddd_glossary.definition: A record of something significant that happened in the domain. Events are immutable facts named in past tense that capture state changes or business-significant occurrences.

audience_notes.auditor: Sagas provide explicit compensation logic — every step has a defined rollback. Audit trails track the full process lifecycle.
audience_notes.data_steward: Cross-context data flow is explicit. Each saga step documents which data moves where.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as saga service with step handlers, compensation logic, timeout management, and state tracking.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Represents a multi-step business process like 'Order Fulfillment' that coordinates across departments.
audience_notes.new_team_member: A recipe for a multi-step process — with clear instructions for what to undo if something goes wrong.
audience_notes.project_manager: Complex business workflows modeled explicitly. Each step, its triggers, and its failure handling are visible in the model.
audience_notes.security_officer: Saga state transitions are logged. Timeout policies prevent indefinite resource holds.

ddd_glossary.definition: A coordination mechanism for multi-step business processes that span multiple aggregates or bounded contexts. Sagas manage compensating actions if a step fails, ensuring eventual consistency.

audience_notes.auditor: Business rules are explicit and named. 'OverdueInvoice' is a defined specification, not ad-hoc SQL.
audience_notes.data_steward: Data access patterns are explicit. Each specification documents what data is queried and why.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as SQL WHERE clause, C++ predicate function, and gRPC query filter parameter.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Named business rules that can be referenced across the system. 'VIPCustomer' means the same everywhere.
audience_notes.new_team_member: A named filter — instead of writing WHERE clauses everywhere, you define them once with a meaningful name.
audience_notes.project_manager: Business rules are documented in the model. Changes to a rule automatically propagate to all consumers.
audience_notes.security_officer: Query filtering through specifications prevents arbitrary SQL injection — all queries are model-defined.

ddd_glossary.definition: A named, reusable predicate that encapsulates a business rule or query condition. Specifications can be combined using logical operators and translate to SQL WHERE clauses.

audience_notes.auditor: All data access goes through a defined interface — no direct database queries bypass the repository.
audience_notes.data_steward: Single point of data access per aggregate. All CRUD operations are model-driven and auditable.
audience_notes.developer: Split into CommandRepository (writes) and QueryRepository (reads) per CQRS. Generated as C++ classes with pqxx.
audience_notes.domain_expert: The system's 'memory' — how it stores and retrieves business objects.
audience_notes.new_team_member: The 'go-between' that handles saving and loading your domain objects to/from the database.
audience_notes.project_manager: Data access is generated, not hand-written. Schema changes automatically update repository code.
audience_notes.security_officer: Repository layer enforces data access policies. All queries are parameterized — no SQL injection.

ddd_glossary.definition: An abstraction that mediates between the domain layer and data mapping layers. Repositories provide a collection-like interface for accessing and persisting aggregates.

audience_notes.auditor: Services are the transaction boundary. All aggregate modifications are orchestrated through services.
audience_notes.data_steward: Data modification policies are enforced by services. All writes are validated and logged.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as CommandService (mutations) and QueryService (reads). All aggregate access goes through services.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Business operations that involve multiple entities — like 'Transfer Funds' between two accounts.
audience_notes.new_team_member: The 'verbs' of the system — operations like 'create patient', 'approve order', 'transfer funds'.
audience_notes.project_manager: Services define the system's API surface. Each operation is a documented, testable entry point.
audience_notes.security_officer: Authentication and authorization are enforced at the service layer. No aggregate bypass.

ddd_glossary.definition: A stateless operation that doesn't naturally belong to any entity or value object. Domain services coordinate aggregate interactions and enforce cross-aggregate business rules.

audience_notes.auditor: Reference data is versioned and seeded from tracked CSV files. Changes are auditable through migration history.
audience_notes.data_steward: Canonical reference data with seed files as the single source of truth.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as PostgreSQL table with UUIDv5 primary keys and constexpr C++ header for compile-time constants.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Standard codes and categories used across the system — like country codes, status types, or priority levels.
audience_notes.new_team_member: Dropdown values — like a list of countries or order statuses — stored in the database and code.
audience_notes.project_manager: Reference data is managed declaratively. Adding a new status code is a CSV edit, not a migration.
audience_notes.security_officer: Lookup values are compile-time constants. No runtime SQL needed for framework-level codes.

ddd_glossary.definition: A table of fixed reference values used for classification and validation. Lookup tables replace CHECK constraints and database ENUMs with a normalized, extensible pattern. Values are seeded from CSV files.

audience_notes.auditor: Shows all inter-system dependencies and data flows. Essential for regulatory compliance mapping.
audience_notes.data_steward: Data lineage across contexts. Shows where data originates and how it flows through the system.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as cross-context gRPC service dependencies and Anti-Corruption Layer adapters.
audience_notes.domain_expert: The 'big picture' of how different business areas connect and share information.
audience_notes.new_team_member: A map of how the different parts of the system talk to each other.
audience_notes.project_manager: Team dependencies are explicit. The context map shows which teams need to coordinate.
audience_notes.security_officer: All cross-context data flows are documented. No hidden integrations or data sharing.

ddd_glossary.definition: A visual and structural representation of how bounded contexts relate to each other. Documents integration patterns (Shared Kernel, Customer-Supplier, Anti-Corruption Layer, etc.) between contexts.

audience_notes.auditor: Consistent terminology across code and documentation reduces miscommunication risk.
audience_notes.data_steward: Data dictionaries are generated from the model. Terminology is consistent across all system layers.
audience_notes.developer: DSL names map directly to class names, table names, and API names. No translation needed.
audience_notes.domain_expert: The code speaks your language. If you call it a 'Prescription', the code calls it 'Prescription' too.
audience_notes.new_team_member: The team agrees on specific words for specific things — and the code uses those exact words.
audience_notes.project_manager: Everyone uses the same terms. The model IS the specification — no separate 'business requirements' document.
audience_notes.security_officer: Unambiguous naming reduces security requirement misinterpretation.

ddd_glossary.definition: A shared language between developers and domain experts, used consistently in code, documentation, and conversation. Within a bounded context, each term has exactly one meaning.

audience_notes.auditor: Write operations and read operations have separate audit trails and access controls.
audience_notes.data_steward: Query models can be denormalized for performance without affecting the authoritative write model.
audience_notes.developer: CommandService/CommandRepository for writes, QueryService/QueryRepository for reads. Generated per aggregate.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Reading data and changing data are handled differently — each optimized for its purpose.
audience_notes.new_team_member: Two separate doors — one for changing data, one for reading it. Each door is optimized for its job.
audience_notes.project_manager: Read and write sides can evolve independently. Reporting views don't affect write performance.
audience_notes.security_officer: Separate authorization for commands vs. queries. Read-only users never see write endpoints.

ddd_glossary.definition: An architectural pattern that separates read operations (queries) from write operations (commands). Each side can be independently optimized, scaled, and secured.

audience_notes.auditor: Business rules enforced at the database level. Invalid data cannot exist, even from direct database access.
audience_notes.data_steward: Data quality rules are model-driven and database-enforced. No invalid data can be persisted.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as PostgreSQL CHECK constraints and C++ validation functions.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Rules that can never be broken — like 'an order must have at least one item' or 'age must be positive'.
audience_notes.new_team_member: Rules that the system enforces automatically — like 'price must be positive'. The database rejects violations.
audience_notes.project_manager: Business rules are in the model, not in code. Changes propagate automatically to database and application.
audience_notes.security_officer: Database-level enforcement means even compromised application code cannot create invalid data.

ddd_glossary.definition: A condition that must always be true for an aggregate to be in a valid state. Invariants are enforced within the aggregate boundary and translate to database CHECK constraints.

audience_notes.auditor: Integration schemas are versioned and documented. Breaking changes are detectable through protobuf compatibility checks.
audience_notes.data_steward: Integration data formats are model-driven and versioned. Schema evolution is tracked.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as .proto files with gRPC service definitions. Backward compatibility enforced by protobuf conventions.
audience_notes.domain_expert: The official 'contract' for how different system parts exchange information.
audience_notes.new_team_member: The 'official form' that different system parts use to send data to each other.
audience_notes.project_manager: API contracts are auto-generated from the model. No separate API specification effort needed.
audience_notes.security_officer: Structured, typed integration format prevents injection attacks and malformed data exchange.

ddd_glossary.definition: A well-documented, versioned schema used for integration between bounded contexts. In Muscovite, Protocol Buffers serve as the published language for inter-context communication.

audience_notes.auditor: Data flow direction is explicit. Supplier changes are traceable to downstream impact.
audience_notes.data_steward: Data lineage: supplier context is the authoritative source, customer context receives derived data.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as gRPC client-server relationship with explicit dependency direction.
audience_notes.domain_expert: One business area provides services to another — like a lab providing test results to a clinical unit.
audience_notes.new_team_member: One part of the system depends on another — like a 'provider and consumer' relationship.
audience_notes.project_manager: Team dependencies are documented. The supplier team knows its downstream consumers.
audience_notes.security_officer: Data flows only in the documented direction. No unauthorized reverse dependencies.

ddd_glossary.definition: A context relationship where one context (supplier/upstream) provides data or services that another context (customer/downstream) depends on. The supplier accommodates the customer's needs.

audience_notes.auditor: Automated business rules with documented triggers and actions. Every policy activation is logged.
audience_notes.data_steward: Automated data transformations are model-driven and traceable to specific business events.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as event handlers that subscribe to specific domain events and invoke commands.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Automatic reactions to business events — like 'when payment is confirmed, ship the order'.
audience_notes.new_team_member: An automatic reaction — 'when this event happens, automatically do that action'.
audience_notes.project_manager: Business automation rules are explicit in the model. No hidden background processes.
audience_notes.security_officer: Policy triggers and actions are documented. No undocumented automated data modifications.

ddd_glossary.definition: An automated reaction to a domain event. Policies listen for specific events and trigger commands or side effects in response, implementing 'when X happens, do Y' business rules.

audience_notes.auditor: Enforces business rules between related fields — e.g., end date must be after start date. All violations are returned as structured error maps.
audience_notes.data_steward: Ensures data consistency across related fields before persistence. Violations are caught at the service layer.
audience_notes.developer: Generated as validate() method on ViewModel (Qt) or CommandService. Returns QVariantMap/std::map of field→error pairs. Override validatePlugin() for Tier 3.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Validates that combinations of fields make business sense — things that single-field rules cannot catch.
audience_notes.new_team_member: Checks that fields work together correctly — like making sure a start date comes before an end date.
audience_notes.project_manager: Cross-field validation is automatically generated from the domain model. No hand-written validation code needed.
audience_notes.security_officer: All cross-field validations are model-driven and deterministic. No user-supplied code runs in Tier 2.

ddd_glossary.definition: A generated class that enforces cross-field invariants within an aggregate. Tier 2 of the three-tier validation architecture (ADR 20260307). Checks relationships between fields that cannot be expressed as single-field constraints.

audience_notes.auditor: User-written business rules with a defined integration point. Changes are tracked in sidecar files under version control.
audience_notes.data_steward: Custom validation logic with access to the full entity state. Runs after generated validators.
audience_notes.developer: Override validatePlugin() in the sidecar .cpp file. Returns QVariantMap of errors which are merged with Tier 2 results. Sentinel comment controls regeneration.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Complex business rules that the domain model language cannot express — like external service lookups or multi-aggregate checks.
audience_notes.new_team_member: The place to write custom validation rules that the code generator doesn't handle — override one method and return errors.
audience_notes.project_manager: Escape hatch for business rules too complex for the DSL. Contained in sidecar files with clear ownership.
audience_notes.security_officer: Plugin code is user-owned and reviewed separately. It runs within the same process boundary as generated code.

ddd_glossary.definition: A user-owned extension point for business rule validation that cannot be expressed in the DSL. Tier 3 of the three-tier validation architecture. Implemented as a virtual method in a sidecar file.

audience_notes.auditor: Structured error reporting with field-level granularity. Every validation failure is attributable to a specific field.
audience_notes.data_steward: Per-field error attribution enables precise data quality feedback to entry operators.
audience_notes.developer: QVariantMap (QML) or std::map<QString,QString> (Widgets). Keys are field names, values are translated error messages.
audience_notes.domain_expert: Users see exactly which fields have problems and why — not just a generic 'form is invalid' message.
audience_notes.new_team_member: A dictionary mapping field names to error messages — the result of calling validate() on a form.
audience_notes.project_manager: Consistent error display across all frontends. Generated automatically from the domain model.
audience_notes.security_officer: Error messages are generated from tr() calls — no user-supplied strings in error output.

ddd_glossary.definition: A structured map of field names to error messages returned by the three-tier validation pipeline. Used by both Qt Widgets and QML frontends to display per-field error feedback.
